questions.prompt: Your checkout endpoint crashes after creating a Stripe PaymentIntent but before saving the ID locally. The user retries. Without protection, what happens?

choices.text: A second PaymentIntent is created and the user is double-charged. The fix: pass an `idempotencyKey` on every Stripe create call — store the key in your DB before the API call; reuse it on retry. Stripe returns the existing PaymentIntent on the second call instead of creating a new one.
Email the user to retry manually.

questions.explanation: Without idempotency keys, every retry creates a fresh operation. With keys: same key = same operation; Stripe returns the cached result. Generate key once per logical operation, store in DB, reuse on retries. 24-hour cache window.

questions.prompt: Stripe delivers a webhook successfully, but your endpoint times out before responding. Stripe retries 5 times. Without protection, what happens?

choices.text: Stripe gives up after one retry.

Your handler processes the same event 5 times — 5 duplicate DB inserts, 5 confirmation emails, etc. The fix: check `event.id` against your DB before processing; if seen, return 200 and skip. End-to-end idempotency is required: Stripe's idempotency keys cover your server-to-Stripe leg; webhook dedup covers Stripe-to-your-server.

questions.explanation: Stripe retries webhooks on failures or timeouts. Without dedup, the same event processes multiple times — duplicate side effects everywhere. Track processed Event IDs in your DB; skip if seen. Pair with idempotency keys for full end-to-end protection.
